companies.name: Meta
companies.summary: Meta publishes per-site community-impact updates through datacenters.atmeta.com, anchored by the Community Action Grants program (funding for local nonprofits and schools in host communities). Each new campus announcement includes paired commitments around STEM education, infrastructure investment, and renewable energy purchases. Long-term framing: data centers as 'long-term community partners' with annual reporting cadence per location.

companies.name: Google
companies.summary: Google's data center community framework lives at datacenters.google, with per-state location pages and detailed economic-impact reports (state GDP contribution, jobs, tax revenue, philanthropic giving). The Data Center Grants program (Google.org) funds local nonprofits in host communities; the STAR program partners with community colleges on data-center workforce training. Public commitments include 24/7 carbon-free energy by 2030 and 120% water replenishment by 2030.

companies.name: Microsoft
companies.summary: Microsoft's 2024 Datacenter Community Pledge formalizes four commitments: advancing community prosperity, addressing local environmental impacts, building skills for the AI economy, and operating in a manner deserving of trust. Per-region community-engagement reports; Datacenter Academy partnerships with community colleges (Wisconsin's Gateway Tech is the flagship). Carbon-negative and water-positive by 2030 commitments operate across the global data center portfolio.

companies.name: Amazon (AWS)
companies.summary: AWS publishes per-state economic impact studies (Virginia, Pennsylvania, Indiana, Ohio) detailing investment, jobs supported, tax revenue, and infrastructure. Community programs include Think Big Spaces (STEM education in 116 schools across 12 countries) and Amazon Community Funds in announcement regions (Pennsylvania, Indiana, etc.). AWS InCommunities is the umbrella for local engagement; per-region announcements highlight local utility, education, and grant commitments.

companies.name: OpenAI
companies.summary: OpenAI does not currently publish a dedicated data-center community-impact framework. The January 2025 Stargate Project announcement framed expected impact in jobs and national-security terms ('over 100,000 American jobs'). Community engagement structure for individual sites — starting with Abilene, TX — has not been publicly established; on-the-ground engagement is being led by the City of Abilene and Texas state officials rather than OpenAI directly.

companies.name: Anthropic
companies.summary: Anthropic does not operate its own data centers and has no published community-impact framework. The company's compute capacity comes via partnerships with AWS (Project Rainier in New Carlisle, IN, ~500K Trainium2 chips) and Google Cloud. Community impact at host sites flows through the operating partner's framework, not Anthropic's — a structural gap for any community advocate trying to engage Anthropic directly on local impact.

companies.name: xAI
companies.summary: xAI has no dedicated company community-impact page. Local commitments for the Memphis Colossus campus are published via the Greater Memphis Chamber, including pledges around jobs (500 initial), local tax revenue ($100M+ projected), water recycling ($80M facility, 4.7B gallons protected annually), and electrical-grid substations ($55M). The Memphis framing emerged largely after public pushback over on-site gas turbines and an NAACP / SELC Clean Air Act notice of intent to sue.

companies.name: Oracle
companies.summary: Oracle does not publish a data-center-specific community-impact framework. OCI economic-impact framing is embedded in broader cloud business marketing rather than per-site community pages. For the Stargate Abilene campus the workload owner is OpenAI; community engagement is being led by the City of Abilene and Texas state officials rather than Oracle directly.

companies.name: Wonder Valley
companies.hq: Boston, MA (O'Leary Digital + West GenCo JV)
companies.summary: Wonder Valley / Stratos has no public community-impact page. Project framing comes via Kevin O'Leary's media appearances (Fox News, KUTV, Deseret News, CNN) and the Feb 2026 PR Newswire joint-venture announcement. The May 2026 development consent agreement with Box Elder County was approved without a completed environmental review; Gov. Cox subsequently announced a Phase 1 cap reducing the project from ~7.5 GW to ~1.5 GW following sustained public pushback from BEAR (Box Elder Accountability Referendum) and other resident groups.

companies.name: QTS
companies.summary: QTS Data Centers, a Blackstone portfolio company since its 2021 take-private acquisition (~$10B), publishes a community framework it calls 'five guarantees that guide how we build, operate and partner in the places we call home.' The framework's headline commitment is paying 100% of the energy costs for QTS facilities so that grid-upgrade and generation costs are not shifted to existing utility ratepayers; QTS publicly signed the White House's March 2026 Ratepayer Protection Pledge. Adjacent commitments include closed-loop water-free cooling at all greenfield builds since 2018, town-hall engagement with host communities, locally-funded scholarship and grant programs (notably a $50M Dane County, WI community commitment), and 'success-based giving' partnerships. QTS's annual sustainability report claims 100% carbon-free operational electricity in 2023 and a 5% annual portfolio Water Utilization Effectiveness reduction target.
